common_name: Green Velvet Alocasia (Frydek, Velvet Alocasia, Micholitziana)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container in zone 9; Alocasia micholitziana cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. It requires higher humidity (50–70%) than many common aroids and will develop brown leaf margins in dry air. Use a well-draining aroid potting mix with perlite; this Alocasia is prone to root rot in heavy soils. The plant may go dormant (dropping all leaves) during winter months in low-light conditions — this is normal; maintain minimal watering until new growth appears. Propagate by separating offset corms from the base. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Green Velvet Alocasia produces its best velvety leaf texture and deepest coloration in consistent bright indirect light and will decline in dim, low-light conditions more quickly than many shade tolerant aroids. Alocasia micholitziana is a compact aroid from the Philippines in the family Araceae, most commonly encountered in commerce under the cultivar name 'Frydek' — a name that has become so attached to the plant that 'Alocasia Frydek' is more widely used in retail than the botanical name. The leaves are the primary ornamental feature: narrow, elongated, arrowhead-shaped, dark emerald-green, and covered with a deep, dense velvety texture produced by papillae that give the leaf a rich, soft, matte surface resembling velvet fabric. The veins are precisely delineated in bright white or silvery-white in a strongly contrasting pattern against the dark velvet surface — a combination that makes the plant one of the most striking of the compact Alocasia species. Green Velvet Alocasia is generally regarded as a somewhat challenging but rewarding houseplant — more demanding than common aroids like Pothos or Philodendron, but significantly more manageable than the giant collector Alocasias. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate crystals.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alocasia species for tropical indoor culture
Royal Horticultural Society — Alocasia micholitziana cultivation
American Horticulture Society — Alocasia 'Frydek': Green Velvet characteristics
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Climbing Bird's Nest Fern (Common Microsorum, Crocodile Fern, Wart Fern)

planting.note: Grow in shade as a houseplant, on a bark mount, or in zone 9b-11 outdoor shade gardens; Microsorum punctatum is more cold-tolerant than Asplenium nidus and can survive brief temperatures to 32°F if the rhizome is protected with mulch. In zone 9-10, plant outdoors in deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil under trees. The plant naturally creeps and climbs surfaces with its surface-creeping rhizomes — allow it to grow up mossy tree trunks or walls for the most natural appearance. Propagate by dividing rhizome sections with attached fronds.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or potting medium; Climbing Bird's Nest Fern is highly adaptable and among the most shade-tolerant of the large structural ferns for zone 9 Gulf Coast outdoor shade gardens and indoor use. Microsorum punctatum (also known as Phymatosorus punctatus) is a widespread, variable epiphytic to terrestrial fern distributed across tropical Africa, Asia, the Pacific Islands, and Australia, growing naturally in forest understories, on mossy tree trunks, and on rock faces. The plant produces long, strap-like, undivided fronds of various sizes (depending on cultivar and conditions) — from 12 to 36 inches — in bright to medium green, with the characteristic small, round, orange-brown sori (spore clusters) scattered across the frond undersurface in a dotted pattern that can be visible from above in strong light. Some forms have attractively crested or wavy frond margins. A popular cultivar, 'Grandiceps,' has strongly crested frond tips. The rhizome creeps across surfaces, attaching and climbing — in zone 9-10 outdoor shade gardens it naturally climbs the trunks of large oaks and palms, eventually producing an impressive colony of ferns ascending tree trunks. More cold-tolerant than Bird's Nest Fern (Asplenium nidus), making it preferable for zone 9 outdoor planting. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Microsorum punctatum for Florida shade gardens
American Fern Society — Microsorum species: cultivated ferns
LSU AgCenter — Shade Ferns for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 9
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Microsorum punctatum species notes

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container in zone 9; Kohleria eriantha cannot tolerate frost. It grows from scaly rhizomes — plant the rhizomes in spring in a well-draining, slightly acidic potting mix and allow the soil to approach dryness between waterings, as the rhizomes are prone to rot in wet soil. After flowering, the plant may die back partially in winter — reduce watering and resume in spring when new growth appears. Propagate from rhizome divisions, stem cuttings, or seed. The fuzzy stems and leaves distinguish Kohleria from similar gesneriads. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Kohleria eriantha flowers most abundantly in bright indirect light and will produce sparse blooms in dim conditions, though it tolerates lower light than many flowering houseplants. Kohleria eriantha is a rhizomatous gesneriad from Colombia and adjacent South America in the family Gesneriaceae, closely related to the African Violet (Saintpaulia) and Gloxinia (Sinningia) in the same plant family. The entire plant — stems, leaves, and calyxes — is covered with soft, reddish or white hairs that give it a distinctive furry, velvety texture (the specific epithet 'eriantha' means 'woolly-flowered'). The leaves are large, broadly oval, dark-green with a soft, hairy texture, arranged in opposite pairs on the upright stems. The tubular flowers are 1–2 inches long, orange-red with a yellow throat spotted with red dots, borne in axillary clusters along the upper portions of the stems over an extended bloom period from spring through fall. Modern Kohleria hybrids (often simply labeled 'Kohleria hybrid') are available in colors ranging from pink and red to orange, yellow, and bicolored forms, all retaining the characteristic fuzzy texture and tubular flower form that makes the genus distinctive in the gesneriad family. An underused but rewarding shade houseplant for zones where humidity can be maintained. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Gesneriad Society — Kohleria eriantha care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Kohleria cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gesneriads for indoor flowering in Florida
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Kohleria (non-toxic)

common_name: Queen Sago (Rumph's Cycad, Coastal Sago, Queen Cycad)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun to partial shade with fast-draining, well-aerated soil; Cycas rumphii is more cold-sensitive than C. revoluta and best suited to zone 9b-11 outdoors. In zone 9a, container culture is safer — mature specimens can be very large containers to move. The plant produces a single flush of new leaves (a 'crown') each spring — protect the emerging fronds from cold and damage during this period. CAUTION: All parts of cycads (seeds, leaves, stems) are highly toxic to animals and humans.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; Queen Sago is highly drought-tolerant once established and requires excellent drainage — it will not survive waterlogged or poorly draining soils. Cycas rumphii is a large, majestic cycad native to the coastal regions of South and Southeast Asia (India, Sri Lanka, Indonesia, Philippines, New Guinea) and the Pacific Islands, where it often grows near coastlines in sandy, well-drained soils close to the sea — a habitat that explains its common salt-tolerance and drought adaptation. Like all cycads, Cycas rumphii is a living fossil, a member of an ancient plant lineage that predates the dinosaurs by more than 100 million years and has changed little since the Jurassic period. The plant produces a stout trunk topped by a crown of long (4–8 feet), arching, pinnately-compound, dark-green, glossy fronds similar to palm fronds. Cycas rumphii is larger and more tropical in appearance than the commonly grown Japanese Sago Palm (Cycas revoluta), with wider, more widely-spaced leaflets and a faster growth rate in warm climates. In zone 9-11 Gulf Coast landscapes, it serves as a dramatic tropical accent and structural focal point. CAUTION: All parts of all cycads, including C. rumphii, contain cycasin and other toxins causing severe liver failure in pets and humans if ingested — plant away from areas accessible to children and pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cycas rumphii for Florida landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Cycas rumphii cultivation notes
Cycad Society — Cycas rumphii species profile and toxicity
LSU AgCenter — Cycads for Gulf Coast Louisiana zone 9 landscapes

common_name: Miniature Wax Plant (Wax Bell, Beautiful Hoya, Hoya bella)

planting.note: Grow as a hanging basket or pendant container in bright indirect light; Hoya lanceolata subsp. bella cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. Unlike most Hoyas, it prefers slightly more consistent moisture (never fully drying out) because of its naturally epiphytic roots in humid forest understory. Use a fine-textured, well-aerated potting mix. Never remove old peduncles. Propagate from stem cuttings in moist perlite or sphagnum. The naturally pendant growth habit makes it ideal for small hanging baskets. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light to moderate shade — Miniature Wax Plant requires more light to bloom than its size suggests; place within 3 feet of a bright window for reliable flowering, while avoiding direct sun which burns the small leaves. Hoya lanceolata subsp. bella (commonly sold simply as 'Hoya bella') is a small, naturally pendant epiphytic Hoya from Myanmar (Burma) and the eastern Himalayas in the family Apocynaceae — one of the most compact and delicate of the commonly grown Hoyas. The slender, arching stems produce pairs of small, lance-shaped to oval, pale to medium-green, slightly succulent leaves about an inch long in an airy, cascading arrangement naturally suited to small hanging baskets and elevated containers. In bloom, the plant produces pendant umbels of 7–12 tiny, perfectly star-shaped, white flowers with a contrasting center corona of deep rose-purple — each individual flower less than half an inch across, but the massed effect of many clusters simultaneously in bloom creates a charming and delicate display. The flowers are lightly but pleasantly fragrant. Unlike the vigorous, twining large Hoyas, H. lanceolata subsp. bella has a naturally arching, pendant, non-climbing growth habit — it does not reach toward a support or twine but simply cascades downward in long, graceful arches. This makes it one of the finest small Hoyas for hanging baskets and elevated windowsill displays. Non-toxic.
sources: American Hoya Society — Hoya lanceolata subsp. bella care guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Hoya lanceolata subsp. bella cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hoya species for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Hoya (non-toxic)
